instruments: Payment Services Act 2019 (Act 2 of 2019, 'PS Act') -- the base licensing statute for 'digital payment token service' (DPT service); confirmed present via direct fetch of the Payment Services (Amendment) Bill (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg), which amends the principal Act's section 2(1) definitions and inserts the custody-empowerment section described below

Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 -- inserted new PS Act section 21A ('Additional requirements in respect of licensee providing digital payment token service'), the statutory hook empowering MAS to make regulations on: commingling of customer assets (s.21A(1)(a)); minimum proportion of customer/own assets to be maintained in a prescribed manner (s.21A(1)(b)); safeguarding of customer assets on the licensee's insolvency (s.21A(1)(c)); insolvency insurance (s.21A(1)(d)); manner of safeguarding customer assets, DPT instruments and customer data (s.21A(1)(e)-(h)); limits on DPT amounts held/transferred (s.21A(1)(i)-(j)); and use of customer moneys/DPTs in the licensee's own business (s.21A(1)(l)-(m)) -- with contravention of most limbs punishable by a fine of up to S$250,000 (s.21A(3)); text confirmed by direct fetch of the Bill PDF (parliament.gov.sg, Bill No. 41/2020, read first time 2 November 2020); the Bill's own short title clause states it becomes the 'Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021' on commencement by Gazette notification -- the final Act number/gazettal citation was not independently re-confirmed against Singapore Statutes Online in this pass because sso.agc.gov.sg returned an HTTP 403 on every path tried (see open_items)

Payment Services Regulations (subsidiary legislation made under PS Act s.103(1)/s.21A) on segregation and safeguarding of DPT-service customers' assets -- per MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release (fetched via sgpc.gov.sg), these regulations 'take effect six months from 4 April 2024' (i.e. 4 October 2024) and require DPT service providers to segregate customers' assets and place them in a trust account for the customers' benefit, maintain proper books and records, and maintain effective systems and controls to protect the integrity and security of customers' assets

MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', 2 April 2024 -- announces that amendments to the PS Act and subsidiary legislation take effect in stages from 4 April 2024, bringing (i) provision of custodial services for DPTs, (ii) facilitation of DPT transmission between accounts and facilitation of DPT exchange (even without the provider taking possession of moneys/DPTs), and (iii) facilitation of cross-border money transfer, within the scope of PS Act regulation; sets transitional deadlines (30-day notification, 6-month licence application, 9-month AML/CFT attestation report, all measured from 4 April 2024); fetched directly as a genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg (Singapore Press Centre), since www.mas.gov.sg serves an identical 'Maintenance' bot-block page on every path tried this session, confirmed live

MAS media release, 'MAS Publishes Investor Protection Measures for Digital Payment Token Services', 3 July 2023 -- announces new requirements for DPT service providers to safekeep customer assets under a statutory trust, restrictions on facilitating lending and staking of DPTs for retail customers (institutional/accredited investors excepted), and publication of a first-tranche consultation response on segregation/custody following an October 2022 public consultation; fetched directly via sgpc.gov.sg

MAS media release, 'MAS Strengthens Regulatory Measures for Digital Payment Token Services', 23 November 2023 -- publishes the final tranche of consultation responses, covering business-conduct measures (conflicts-of-interest disclosure, listing policies, complaints handling), consumer-access measures (no financing/margin/leverage transactions, no local credit-card payments, discouraging retail speculation), and technology/cyber-risk requirements for DPT service providers, to take effect in phases from mid-2024; fetched directly via sgpc.gov.sg

Guidelines on Provision of Consumer Protection Safeguards by Digital Payment Token Service Providers [PS-G03] -- MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release footnotes that MAS 'has also published Guidelines on implementation measures to safeguard customers' assets', confirming the guidelines' existence and their asset-safeguarding subject matter as a primary-source fact; a 19 September 2024 revision date and specific chapter effective dates (4 October 2024 for the safeguarding-personnel provision; 19 June 2025 for consumer-access, conflict-of-interest and business-conduct chapters) are reported by secondary legal-commentary sources only in this pass, since every direct fetch attempt against www.mas.gov.sg (including the guideline's own -/media/ PDF path) returned the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page rather than the guideline text -- see open_items

Securities and Futures Act 2001 (SFA) and Financial Advisers Act 2001 (FAA) -- apply, in place of the PS Act, where a digital token is a 'digital representation of capital markets products'; confirmed via direct fetch of MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release ('MAS Clarifies Regulatory Regime for Digital Token Service Providers', via sgpc.gov.sg), whose footnote 1 states verbatim: 'As digital tokens comprise digital payment tokens and digital representation of capital markets products, the provision of digital token services in Singapore would be regulated under the Payment Services Act 2019, Securities and Futures Act 2001 or Financial Advisers Act 2001'

Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 (FSM Act), Part 9 -- the Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) regime, commenced 30 June 2025, licensing persons who provide digital-token dealing/custody/advisory-type services from Singapore solely to customers outside Singapore; MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release (fetched via sgpc.gov.sg) cites its own Annex definitions and licensing provisions as FSM Act 'Section 136(1)' (definitions of 'digital token', 'digital token service', 'digital token service provider') and 'Section 137' (licensing of digital token service providers); the release states providers already serving Singapore customers 'are already subject to regulation' under the PS Act/SFA/FAA and face 'no change to what the licensed providers can do', while providers serving only customers outside Singapore must cease that activity from 30 June 2025 absent a licence, with MAS stating it 'will generally not issue a licence' for that category given elevated money-laundering risk and supervisory reach -- this pillar records the DTSP regime's existence and commencement date only as they bear on dealing/custody/advisory scope; the regime's AML/CFT substance is tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar to avoid duplicating citations
standing_summary: Singapore regulates dealing, custody and advisory activity in digital assets through a single integrated regulator, the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS), acting under three statutes rather than a dedicated crypto-custody or crypto-advisory law: the Payment Services Act 2019 (PS Act) for digital payment tokens (DPTs), the Securities and Futures Act 2001 (SFA) and Financial Advisers Act 2001 (FAA) where a token is a capital-markets product, and, since 30 June 2025, the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 (FSM Act) Part 9 Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) regime for token-related services performed from Singapore for customers located solely outside it. Custody: the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 inserted PS Act section 21A, empowering MAS to prescribe requirements on commingling of customer assets, minimum asset-maintenance ratios, safeguarding customer assets on a licensee's insolvency, insolvency insurance, and the manner and disclosure of asset/data safeguarding -- confirmed by direct fetch of the enabling Bill's text (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg), which is itself a genuinely fetchable primary source distinct from MAS's own blocked website and from Singapore Statutes Online, which returned an HTTP 403 on every path tried this session (site root, a specific Act page, robots.txt and sitemap.xml alike), consistent with this jurisdiction's earlier live-verified finding that sso.agc.gov.sg cannot serve as a quote-source, only a link. MAS exercised that section 21A power through Payment Services Regulations that took effect 4 October 2024 (six months after the enabling PS Act amendments commenced 4 April 2024), requiring DPT service providers to segregate customers' assets into a trust account held for customers' benefit, maintain proper books and records, and maintain effective systems and controls -- this timeline and substance is confirmed directly from MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release, fetched as a genuine PDF via the Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg) rather than MAS's own domain, which served an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance'-titled HTML page to every path tried this session including the direct -/media/ PDF path for the corresponding guideline document. That same media release confirms the amendments bring three activities within PS Act scope from 4 April 2024: provision of custodial services for DPTs; facilitation of DPT transmission between accounts and facilitation of DPT exchange, including where the provider never takes possession of the moneys or DPTs; and facilitation of cross-border money transfer even where moneys are not received in Singapore -- with transitional deadlines of 30 days (MAS notification), six months (licence application) and nine months (AML/CFT attestation report) all measured from 4 April 2024, and entities that fail to meet these required to cease the newly regulated activities. The segregation/trust-account policy itself was first announced in MAS's 3 July 2023 media release (also fetched via sgpc.gov.sg), which stated DPT service providers would be required to safekeep customer assets under a statutory trust 'before the end of the year' (2023) following an October 2022 public consultation, and that MAS would restrict DPT service providers from facilitating lending and staking of DPTs for retail customers (institutional and accredited investors excepted) because such activities are 'generally not suitable for retail public'. A final tranche of related measures was published in MAS's 23 November 2023 media release (also fetched via sgpc.gov.sg): business-conduct requirements (conflict-of-interest disclosure, published listing policies, complaint-handling procedures), consumer-access measures (no financing/margin/leverage transactions for retail DPT trading, no local credit-card payment acceptance, no trading incentives, discouraging speculation), and minimum technology/cyber-risk requirements, all to take effect in phases from mid-2024. MAS also references, in its 2 April 2024 release's own footnote, having 'published Guidelines on implementation measures to safeguard customers' assets' -- understood to be Guidelines on Provision of Consumer Protection Safeguards by Digital Payment Token Service Providers [PS-G03] -- confirming the guidelines' existence and subject matter as a primary-source fact, though this pillar could not independently fetch the guideline document itself this session (every www.mas.gov.sg path, including the guideline's own PDF URL, served the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page); a reported 19 September 2024 revision and specific chapter effective dates (4 October 2024 for safeguarding-personnel provisions; 19 June 2025 for consumer-access, conflict-of-interest and business-conduct chapters) rest on secondary legal-commentary sources only in this pass and should be independently re-verified before being treated as primary-confirmed (see open_items). Dealing and advisory in capital-markets-product tokens: MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release ('MAS Clarifies Regulatory Regime for Digital Token Service Providers', fetched via sgpc.gov.sg) states in its own footnote that 'digital tokens comprise digital payment tokens and digital representation of capital markets products', and that provision of digital token services in Singapore is regulated 'under the Payment Services Act 2019, Securities and Futures Act 2001 or Financial Advisers Act 2001' depending on which category applies -- meaning dealing in, or advising on, a token that is itself a capital markets product (e.g. a tokenised security) falls to the SFA/FAA capital-markets-services and financial-adviser licensing regimes on the same technology-neutral basis as their non-tokenised counterparts, rather than to the PS Act. That release also confirms the newer FSM Act Part 9 DTSP regime, commenced 30 June 2025, citing its own Annex as sourcing the 'digital token'/'digital token service'/'digital token service provider' definitions to FSM Act section 136(1) and the licensing requirement to FSM Act section 137: providers serving customers in Singapore for token dealing/custody/advisory-type services 'are already subject to regulation' under the PS Act/SFA/FAA framework described above with 'no change to what the licensed providers can do', while providers whose token-related services are performed from Singapore solely for customers located outside Singapore must hold a DTSP licence from 30 June 2025 or cease that activity, with MAS stating in the release that it 'will generally not issue a licence' for that specific cross-border-only category, citing elevated money-laundering risk and limited supervisory reach; MAS reported being aware of 'a very small number' of affected providers. Whether the DTSP regime's own scope, beyond the licensing hook itself, specifically extends to token-advisory services (as distinct from dealing/custody/exchange services) and how that interacts with the FAA's own 'investment products' definition, is corroborated only by secondary law-firm commentary in this pass and not independently confirmed against the FSM Act's own Part 9 or FAA definitional text, since the enabling Financial Services and Markets (Amendment) Bill (Bill No. 11/2023, parliament.gov.sg) returned an HTTP 403 on direct fetch this session, unlike the earlier Payment Services (Amendment) Bill which was genuinely fetchable from the same parliament.gov.sg domain -- see open_items. This pillar covers custody, DPT/capital-markets-product dealing, and advisory regulation only; the digital-payment-token-service licensing regime itself (standard/major payment institution categories, advertising restrictions, Investor Alert List) is tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar; AML/CFT duties applicable to custody and dealing intermediaries, including the DTSP regime's AML/CFT substance and MAS Notice PSN02, are tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar -- not restated here to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.

key_links.label: Parliament of Singapore: Payment Services (Amendment) Bill, Bill No. 41/2020 (read first time 2 November 2020) -- official PDF; source of new PS Act section 21A custody/safeguarding text quoted above; fetch-verified live this session

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', 2 April 2024 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session
Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Publishes Investor Protection Measures for Digital Payment Token Services', 3 July 2023 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session
Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Strengthens Regulatory Measures for Digital Payment Token Services', 23 November 2023 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Clarifies Regulatory Regime for Digital Token Service Providers', 6 June 2025 -- official PDF, source of the SFA/FAA footnote and FSM Act s.136(1)/s.137 citations quoted above; fetch-verified live this session

MAS: Guidelines on Consumer Protection Measures by DPT Service Providers [PS-G03] -- link only; every path tried on www.mas.gov.sg (including this guideline's own PDF path) served an identical 'Maintenance' bot-block page to this project's honest User-Agent, plain curl and WebFetch alike, verified this session; cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg): Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 -- link only; returned an HTTP 403 CloudFront error on every path tried this session (including robots.txt); cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate, listed here for reference only

MAS: 'Guide on the Tokenisation of Capital Markets Products' -- link only; www.mas.gov.sg served the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page on this path this session; relevant to SFA/FAA applicability for tokenised capital-markets products but not independently fetched as primary text

open_items: This card relies on MAS's own media releases (fetched as genuine PDFs via sgpc.gov.sg, the Singapore Press Centre) rather than MAS's own website, since every path tried against www.mas.gov.sg this session -- including direct -/media/ PDF paths for the PS-G03 guideline and the Tokenisation of Capital Markets Products guide -- served an identical 853,817-byte HTML page titled 'Maintenance' with noindex,nofollow, to this project's honest User-Agent, plain curl, and WebFetch alike. As a result, the guideline documents themselves (PS-G03 and the CMP tokenisation guide) are cited here only via secondary references inside MAS's own fetched media releases, not by direct quotation of the guideline text. Should mas.gov.sg become fetchable, these guidelines should be independently fetched and quoted directly.

The reported 19 September 2024 revision date for PS-G03 and its chapter-specific effective dates (4 October 2024 for safeguarding-personnel provisions under paragraph 5.3; 19 June 2025 for the consumer-access, conflict-of-interest and business-conduct chapters) rest on secondary legal-commentary sources (law-firm bulletins) in this pass, not on direct fetch of the guideline PDF itself, which was blocked. Should be independently re-confirmed once mas.gov.sg is fetchable or an alternate genuinely primary channel is found.

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg) -- the authoritative source for the consolidated Payment Services Act 2019 text (including section 21A as enacted, with any subsequent amendments) and for the enacted, gazetted citation of the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 -- returned an HTTP 403 CloudFront error on every path tried this session, including robots.txt. This card's account of section 21A is instead drawn from direct fetch of the enabling Bill (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg), which is bill text, not the consolidated Act; the current, post-enactment consolidated statutory text was not independently confirmed this session and should be re-verified once sso.agc.gov.sg is fetchable.

The Financial Services and Markets (Amendment) Bill (Bill No. 11/2023, parliament.gov.sg), which inserted the Part 9 DTSP regime into the FSM Act, returned an HTTP 403 on direct fetch this session (unlike the earlier Payment Services (Amendment) Bill, No. 41/2020, from the same parliament.gov.sg domain, which was genuinely fetchable). This card's account of FSM Act Part 9's scope -- specifically, whether it extends to token-advisory services as distinct from dealing/custody/exchange services, and how that interacts with the Financial Advisers Act's 'investment products' definition -- is corroborated only by secondary law-firm commentary and by MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release's citation of section numbers (136(1), 137) in its Annex, not by independent reading of the Bill or Act's full definitional text. Should be re-verified against the Bill or the consolidated FSM Act text directly.

This pillar's brief flagged the need to confirm whether ACRA (company registration) and the Singapore Police Force / Commercial Affairs Department (criminal enforcement) have any independent digital-asset regulatory role separate from MAS. This pillar's research did not investigate that question, since neither body's function (if any) bears on dealing, custody or advisory regulation specifically; it is left to whichever pillar (most likely AML/CFT & Enforcement) carries the jurisdiction-wide regulator-structure finding, to avoid duplicating that research across every pillar's card.

This pillar covers custody, DPT/capital-markets-product dealing, and advisory regulation only. The digital-payment-token-service licensing regime itself (licence categories, advertising restrictions under PS-G02, the Investor Alert List) is tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar; AML/CFT duties applicable to custody and dealing intermediaries, including the DTSP regime's AML/CFT substance and MAS Notice PSN02, are tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar -- not restated here to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.
